text: Last class you matched the register of an email to its recipient.
Today you condense a forty-page report into a half-page summary.

Deliverable
Revenue was increased by twelve percent.
The targets were met ahead of schedule.
It was agreed that the budget would be approved.
The decision to enter the market followed the analysis.
Here is my draft summary. The first finding says: I increased revenue and the team cut the costs.
For a briefing, keep it impersonal. The board does not need to know who did it. Put it in the passive.
Understood. Revenue was increased by twelve percent, and the costs were reduced. The targets were met.
Much better. Now shorten the recommendation. Instead of We decided to scale, just write the decision to scale.
So: the decision to scale channel two is recommended, and the full report is in the attachment.
That is an executive summary. Half a page, impersonal, and the board reads it in thirty seconds. Send it.
The objective was to assess the risk of a new market.
Revenue was increased and the targets were met.
The decision to scale the channel is recommended.
Let me summarize the findings in five lines.
The full report is in the attachment.

Hi Eduardo, it is Sarah. The board does not have time to read the full forty-page report, so they have asked for an executive summary. Please boil it down to half a page. Start with one line of background, so they know why the report exists. Then give the objective, what the report set out to answer. After that, list the main findings in plain numbers, and remember the rule for a briefing: keep it impersonal. Do not write I increased revenue or the team cut costs. Write revenue was increased and costs were reduced. The focus is on the result, not on the person. Close with one clear recommendation and the scope of what happens next. Keep it to the key points. If they want the detail, the full report is in the attachment.

Let me think about how to condense this. The full report runs forty pages, but the summary must fit on one. The trick is nominalization: instead of a long clause, I use a single noun. We decided to enter a new market becomes the decision to enter a new market. The team analyzed the data becomes the analysis of the data. One noun replaces a whole sentence, and the briefing gets shorter and sharper. The second trick is the passive voice for reporting. In a formal summary I do not say who did what. The targets were met. The budget was approved. The risks were assessed. It sounds objective, and it puts the result in front of the reader. A good executive summary is read in thirty seconds, and after those thirty seconds the reader knows the background, the findings, and the recommendation.

First, you give one line of background, so the reader knows why the report exists. Then you state the objective, what the report set out to answer. After that, you report the main findings, using the passive voice to stay impersonal. Next, you give one clear recommendation. Finally, you point the reader to the full report in the attachment for the detail.
